steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls after "increasing ______ Washington Dulles..." The sentence before the blank is an independent clause ("From 2009 to 2019, travelers flew out of major urban airports with increasing [frequency]"), and the sentence after is also an independent clause ("Washington Dulles... saw a 6.9 percent increase..."). We need punctuation that legally separates two independent clauses and introduces an example.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'frequency, for example,': A comma before "for example" joins two independent clauses with only a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'frequency, for example;': A comma before an introductory phrase and a semicolon after it is backwards and creates a fragment between them. ✗
  • Choice C — 'frequency, for example.': Same comma splice problem as A; the period ends after "for example" and leaves "Washington Dulles..." dangling without a subject opener. ✗
  • Choice D — 'frequency. For example,': A period ends the first independent clause cleanly. "For example," then opens the second sentence as a transitional phrase followed by the comma that standard usage requires. Two independent clauses, properly separated. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
